title: Quick check: install failure, AV conflict

questions.prompt: What's the right-fix path for an AV-blocked install?

choices.label: Ask the customer to disable AV during the install window.
choices.rationale: Removes coverage during install and trains the customer to disable AV when something gets in the way.

choices: Apply the documented AV exclusions for the specific AV product.

choices.label: Switch the customer to a different AV product.
choices.rationale: Wildly out of scope. Deployment-strategy decision, not a tech fix.

choices.label: Re-run the install three times and hope it works on the fourth.

questions.explanation: Pushed via the AV management console. AV and Huntress are designed to coexist; the exclusions are the contract.

questions.prompt: Which symptom pattern most cleanly points at AV interference rather than another failure class?
choices: RMM exit non-zero with 'access denied' or 'file in use' on the agent binary itself, possibly with the binary quarantined;

choices.label: Service-creation operations failing with policy-shaped event log entries under AppLocker or CodeIntegrity.
Service running, install logs showing repeated 'TCP connection refused' against Huntress hostnames.
Installer exits with 'unsupported OS version' message.

questions.explanation: Sometimes the service starts briefly then dies. AV interference touches the agent binary or service directly; the install log shows it in plain text.

questions.prompt: Why is 'adding exclusions only to the endpoint locally' called out as wrong?
choices: It survives until the next AV policy refresh and then evaporates.

choices.label: Huntress can detect local-only exclusions and refuses to install.
Local exclusions break Huntress's audit log.
Local exclusions trigger Critical incidents in the SOC queue.

questions.explanation: Push exclusions via the AV management console. Local exclusions on managed endpoints are temporary by design; policy refreshes wipe them.

questions.prompt: 'Let me try the install again' five times in a row is the signature of what mistake?
choices: Diagnosing by trial and error rather than reading the install logs.

choices.label: Aggressively warming up the AV's heuristics so it learns the binary.
Following the documented runbook.
choices.rationale: The runbook would have you read the logs first.

choices.label: Practising the install procedure under load.
choices.rationale: Misframes the behaviour as deliberate practice.

questions.explanation: The install logs name the failure mode in plain text. Retries without reading them are a substitute for diagnosis.

questions.prompt: A push to a workstation fails with exit code 1 and 'access denied' in the output. The customer runs Bitdefender, managed via their own console (not yours). What's your first step?

choices.label: Ask the customer's IT to disable Bitdefender on the endpoint while you re-run the install.
choices.rationale: Removes AV coverage during the install window and trains them to reach for 'disable AV' as a normal step.

choices: Pull the Huntress install log from the endpoint to confirm AV interference.

choices.label: Re-run the install three more times and hope it works on the fourth.
choices.rationale: AV behaviour is not random; if it blocked once, it'll block again.

questions.explanation: Then ask the customer's IT to apply the documented Huntress exclusions in their Bitdefender console. Confirm the cause from the logs, propose the documented fix to the right party (the customer's IT), don't ask them to lower coverage.

questions.prompt: The customer's IT applies the exclusions and confirms the policy is in place. You re-run the install. The RMM reports success. After 20 minutes, the agent is not in the portal. What's the right diagnostic?

choices.label: Assume the AV is still blocking and ask for more exclusions.
choices.rationale: The RMM exit code is now clean; the failure has moved on.

choices: Check the agent's local logs to see whether the first phone-home attempt succeeded.

choices.label: Re-push the install.
choices.rationale: Re-pushing an already-installed agent risks duplicate state. The install succeeded; the question is why it didn't register.

questions.explanation: Check the network path (lesson 17 territory). Treat the install failure and the registration failure as separate problems with separate diagnostics, even when they appear back-to-back.

questions.prompt: The customer's AV management console is one your MSP doesn't manage. What's the escalation guidance?
choices: That's a customer-side change request, not a tech fix. Bump.

choices.label: Push the exclusions yourself via PowerShell on each endpoint.
choices.rationale: Per-endpoint manual exclusions are the named anti-pattern.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket and let them coordinate with the customer's AV vendor.
Tell the customer to switch AV products.

questions.explanation: The senior owns the customer-side change conversation.

questions.prompt: The customer asks to be left without Huntress on a specific endpoint 'because the AV is enough.' What's the right move?

choices.label: Agree, the customer is right that AV provides coverage.
choices.rationale: Different layers. The tech doesn't agree or disagree; the senior owns the conversation.

choices.label: Push Huntress anyway and let the senior sort it out later.
choices.rationale: Acting before the layer-that-decides has weighed in.

choices.label: Disable the AV exclusions and re-push to prove a point.

questions.explanation: Coverage decisions are commercial/scope, not tech-level.

questions.prompt: Order the steps for resolving an AV-blocked install.
items: Confirm AV is the cause from the agent's install log (and the AV's own logs if available).
Identify which AV product is on the endpoint.
Apply the documented exclusions for that AV product via the AV management console.
Wait for the exclusion policy to apply on the endpoint.
Re-run the Huntress install from the RMM targeted at the same endpoint.
questions.explanation: Confirm cause, identify product, push exclusions at the management console, wait, retry once, verify in the portal.

questions.prompt: Which detail should the PSA record after resolving an AV-blocked install?
choices: Which AV product was on the endpoint and the exclusion path used.

choices.label: The endpoint's BIOS version.
Screenshots of the AV's UI showing the exclusions.
The customer's IT contact's calendar availability.

questions.explanation: So the next tech doesn't repeat the diagnosis.
